---
title: "4.1.2 Operating systems"
subtitle: "Multitasking and user accounts · P159-P160"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.74em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.34em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)

## Multitasking

### More than one process at a time

<span class="term">Multitasking</span> allows computers to carry out more than one task, or process, at a time.

Each process shares hardware resources under the control of the operating system.

The operating system constantly monitors the status of each process so the processes do not clash.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 4.10 Multitasking diagram screenshot placeholder
:::

## Pre-emptive Multitasking

### Time limits, interrupts and priorities

The textbook identifies these three bullet points as <span class="term">pre-emptive multitasking</span>:

::: {.tight-list}
- resources are allocated to a process for a specific time limit
- the process can be interrupted while it is running
- the process is given a priority so it can have resources according to its priority
:::

The risk is that a low-priority process could be starved of resources.

## Multitasking Management

### Making effective use of CPU time

Using multitasking management:

::: {.tight-list}
- main memory is better managed
- HDD/SSD is better managed
- virtual memory is better managed
- CPU time is used more effectively
:::

## User Accounts

### Separate data and protected accounts

Computers allow more than one user to log onto the system.

Users' data is stored in separate parts of memory for security reasons.

Each user account is protected by a user name and password.

## User Accounts

### What each user can do

The operating system manages different user accounts. This allows each user to:

::: {.tight-list}
- customise their screen layout and other settings
- use separate folders and files
- manage these folders and files themselves
:::

An administrator can create accounts, delete accounts and restrict account activity.

## Multi-user Systems

### Multi-access levels

On large university or industrial computers, the operating system may oversee several user accounts in a complex multi-user system.

The operating system maintains accounts for several users and manages different types of data.

<span class="mark">Multi-access levels</span> permit this control to take place.

## Classroom Check

### Explain multitasking and user accounts

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- multitasking allows more than one process at a time
- the operating system monitors process status to prevent clashes
- pre-emptive multitasking uses time limits, interrupts and priorities
- multitasking makes effective use of CPU time
- user accounts separate user data and use usernames and passwords
- administrators can create, delete and restrict accounts
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Running of applications

The next topic explains hardware, firmware and operating systems when running application software.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 4 Directory / 返回 Unit 4 目录](../../index.html)
